title: Borrowing memory that's being used: catalytic approaches to the Tree Evaluation Problem
speaker: James Cook
abstract: I'll be presenting some joint work with Ian Mertz scheduled to appear at STOC 2020. The study of the Tree Evaluation Problem (TEP), introduced by S. Cook et al. (TOCT 2012), is a promising approach to separating L from P. Given a label in [k] at each leaf of a complete binary tree and an explicit function [k]^2 -> [k] for recursively computing the value of each internal node from its children, the problem is to compute the value at the root node.

proof_stages.label: Complexity motivation and Tree Evaluation Problem setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates TEP as a candidate route toward separating log space from polynomial time, defines the complete-binary-tree evaluation problem, and records the parameters h, k and input size.

proof_stages.label: Branching programs and pebbling as the baseline model
proof_stages.summary: Layered branching programs are introduced as the computational model, pebbling games are used to model remembered node values, and the standard h-pebble strategy yields a branching program of roughly k^h states while the tight pebbling lower bound motivates a matching-state conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Lower-bound targets and restricted-model obstacles
proof_stages.summary: The speaker translates a log-space lower bound into a branching-program state lower bound, compares the pebbling and new algorithms against that target, and explains that known k^h lower bounds only hold under read-once or thrifty restrictions that the new algorithm violates.

proof_stages.label: Catalytic memory and clean reversible register computations
proof_stages.summary: Catalytic-space results motivate the idea of borrowed memory, after which the speaker formalizes reversible ring-register instructions, clean computation of a function into a register, inversion to compute negatives, and a lemma converting such instruction sequences into branching programs.

proof_stages.label: Clean multiplication with borrowed registers
proof_stages.summary: A product-computation lemma shows how two clean subroutines can be interleaved with reversible add/subtract operations so as to cleanly compute f1f2 into a third register using four subroutine calls while allowing all recursive calls to reuse the same registers.

proof_stages.label: Indicator-polynomial CheckNode algorithm
proof_stages.summary: TEP is expressed over F2 by an indicator formula for whether a node has value x, and the first recursive CheckNode algorithm implements this formula with three one-bit registers but incurs too many recursive calls, giving a branching program worse than pebbling.

proof_stages.label: Parallel one-hot computation of all value indicators
proof_stages.summary: The algorithm is improved by storing a k-bit one-hot vector for a node value and computing all value indicators in parallel via a function of two vector registers, yielding branching programs of size 2^{O(k+h)} and beating pebbling when h is much larger than k/log k.

proof_stages.label: Hybrid encoding tradeoff and final upper bound
proof_stages.summary: By interpolating between one-hot and binary encodings with a tunable parameter, the final algorithm achieves about (k/(h+1))^{5h} times a polynomial in k states, beating pebbling for h growing faster than k^{4/5}, and the remaining bottleneck is identified as expensive d-ary clean products.
